name: COMPONENTS
subtitle: Building Blocks
description: Components are self-contained units of UI — a template, styles, and logic bundled together.

questions.q: What decorator marks a class as an Angular component?
questions.explanation: @Component provides metadata (template, styles, selector) so Angular can render the class.

questions.q: Which property defines an inline HTML template in @Component?
questions.explanation: 'template' holds the inline HTML string. Use 'templateUrl' to reference an external file.

questions.q: What is the 'selector' used for?
questions.explanation: The selector (e.g. 'app-root') is the custom HTML tag used to place the component.

questions.q: Which lifecycle hook runs once after inputs are first set?
questions.explanation: ngOnInit fires once after the first ngOnChanges — perfect for initialization logic.

questions.q: Angular two-way binding syntax is called:
questions.explanation: [(ngModel)] — 'banana in a box' — combines property binding [] and event binding ().

name: DIRECTIVES
subtitle: DOM Manipulation
description: Directives extend HTML. Structural directives reshape the DOM; attribute directives change appearance.

questions.q: Which structural directive conditionally shows an element?
questions.explanation: *ngIf adds or removes an element from the DOM based on a truthy expression.

questions.q: *ngFor is used to:
questions.explanation: *ngFor loops over a collection and stamps out a template for each item.

questions.q: Which directive dynamically applies CSS classes?
questions.explanation: [ngClass] is an attribute directive binding an object of class names to true/false values.

questions.q: Structural directives use which prefix in templates?
questions.explanation: The asterisk (*) is syntactic sugar for Angular's <ng-template> desugaring mechanism.

questions.q: Which directive sets inline styles dynamically?
questions.explanation: [ngStyle] lets you bind an object of CSS properties directly to an element.

name: SERVICES & DI
subtitle: Injection Protocol
description: Services hold shared logic. Angular's Dependency Injection system provides them wherever needed.

questions.q: What decorator makes a class injectable?
questions.explanation: @Injectable marks the class so Angular's DI system can instantiate and inject it.

questions.q: What does providedIn: 'root' mean?
questions.explanation: providedIn: 'root' registers the service in the root injector — one shared instance for the whole app.

questions.q: How do you inject a service into a component?
questions.explanation: Declare the service as a typed constructor parameter and Angular's DI injects it automatically.

questions.q: HttpClient comes from which module?
questions.explanation: HttpClientModule (or provideHttpClient()) provides the HttpClient service.

questions.q: RxJS Observables are ideal for:
questions.explanation: Observables represent async streams — perfect for HTTP calls, user events, and state changes.

name: PIPES
subtitle: Transform the Stream
description: Pipes transform display values in templates — format dates, currencies, strings and more.

questions.q: How do you apply a pipe in a template?
questions.explanation: The pipe operator | chains transformations: {{ birthday | date:'short' }}

questions.q: Which built-in pipe formats a number as currency?
questions.explanation: The currency pipe formats: {{ price | currency:'USD' }} → $1,234.00

questions.q: A 'pure' pipe only re-runs when:
questions.explanation: Pure pipes (default) re-run only on reference changes — much better for performance.

questions.q: Which pipe converts a string to uppercase?
questions.explanation: {{ 'hello' | uppercase }} → 'HELLO'. There's also the lowercase pipe.

questions.q: What decorator creates a custom pipe?
questions.explanation: @Pipe({ name: 'myPipe' }) marks a class as a pipe. Implement the transform() method.

name: ROUTING
subtitle: Navigate the Matrix
description: Angular Router maps URLs to components, enabling single-page navigation with deep linking.

questions.q: Which directive renders the matched route's component?
questions.explanation: <router-outlet> is the placeholder where the routed component is inserted.

questions.q: How do you navigate programmatically?
questions.explanation: Inject Router and call this.router.navigate(['/path']) for imperative navigation.

questions.q: A route guard is used for:
questions.explanation: Guards like CanActivate control whether a user can enter or leave a route.

questions.q: How do you define a route parameter?
questions.explanation: { path: 'user/:id' } — access the value via ActivatedRoute.params or snapshot.params.

questions.q: Lazy loading improves apps by:
questions.explanation: loadChildren splits modules into separate bundles that only download when the route is visited.

name: FORMS
subtitle: Reactive Power
description: Angular offers Template-driven and Reactive forms — both provide validation, state, and value access.

questions.q: The two Angular form approaches are:
questions.explanation: Template-driven uses directives in templates; Reactive uses FormGroup/FormControl in the component class.

questions.q: Which class represents a single form field in Reactive Forms?
questions.explanation: FormControl tracks the value and validation state of a single input element.

questions.q: Which module must be imported for Reactive Forms?
questions.explanation: ReactiveFormsModule provides FormGroup, FormControl, FormArray and related directives.

questions.q: How do you mark a field as required in Reactive Forms?
questions.explanation: new FormControl('', Validators.required) — Validators is a utility class with common validators.

questions.q: Which property is true when validators fail?
questions.explanation: control.invalid is true when validators fail. Pair with control.touched to show error messages.
